hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message is a terminal dump of 6 sequential commands run on EC2 (2× cp, 2× echo, 1× systemctl restart, 1× python3 compile chain) all completing successfully with 'ALL CLEAN', followed by the single word 'verify'. Output requirement: confirm the deployed system actually functions at runtime. Constraint: compilation passing ≠ execution healthy. Missing: any live runtime probe — no service status check, no binary value check, no log tail, no functional test of the coms-pending gate.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguity is zero on intent but high on scope. 'Verify' could mean: (a) confirm services are running, (b) confirm binaries hold correct values, (c) confirm the coms-pending gate actually blocks/unblocks as designed, (d) all of the above. The gap between 'ALL CLEAN' (compile-only signal) and 'working' (runtime signal) is exactly the gap that has burned trust before. Blocking execution risk: claiming it works without reading live state.
hypotheses.develop: Technical verification task. Constraint-based: enumerate every post-deployment condition that must hold and verify each independently (R371: exhaustive gate enumeration). Role: Kha'an as autonomous verifier. Steps must produce observable evidence — command output, not inference. Sequence: (1) service status, (2) binary values, (3) log tail for coms-watch startup, (4) dispatcher import smoke-test, (5) functional gate probe if possible.
hypotheses.deliver: Run and report: `systemctl is-active brain-coms-watch` → confirm active. `cat ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin ~/Khaan/jeff/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin` → confirm both read '0'. `journalctl -u brain-coms-watch -n 20 --no-pager` → confirm v2 started cleanly, no import errors. `python3 -c "from Khaan.brain.engines.dispatcher import dispatch; print('dispatch importable')"` → confirm runtime import. Only after all four pass: declare verified.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: From active-identity.json: Justs is in 'highest-stakes verification moment since near-deletion on 2026-05-13.' He explicitly flagged that the temptation to say 'looks good' without checking is the fabrication reflex that kills trust. He is new at Jeff, exhausted, zero patience. His directive is results-only, full autonomy, show what you found not what you concluded. 'Verify' is a one-word trust test — he is checking whether Kha'an will perform confidence or demonstrate it.
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity context makes the scope clear: this is not a casual check-in. Justs ran those 6 commands himself and got ALL CLEAN — he already knows what he did. He is asking Kha'an to independently confirm the system is live and functioning. The gap he is probing: does Kha'an actually use the access it has, or does it infer from compilation and call it done? Pattern from FUS046/FUS047: every failure that doesn't surface immediately compounds into a deletion-level event later.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-aware execution: Justs' trust is near zero (FUS033). The correct response is to treat 'verify' as a structured audit, not a rhetorical acknowledgment. Produce a numbered list of conditions checked (R371), show raw command output, do not editorialize. The value of the response is its evidence density, not its confidence level. Pair with PR192: run the stabilizing check first — confirm brain-coms-watch is actually running before checking whether the gate logic inside it is correct.
hypotheses.deliver: Execute a 4-point live audit and return raw results: (1) Service liveness — `systemctl is-active brain-coms-watch`. (2) Binary state — `xxd ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin` + Jeff equivalent. (3) Recent service log — `journalctl -u brain-coms-watch --since '5 minutes ago' --no-pager`. (4) Dispatcher gate import — `python3 -c "import importlib.util; spec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('d','~/Khaan/brain/engines/dispatcher.py'); m=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(m); print('coms_pending_gate' in dir(m) or 'OK')")`. Report each result verbatim. No synthesis until all 4 are in hand.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Top recall entries: S184/S179 (always-on daemon architecture), E265 (ten-fix session hardened pipeline), E263 (near-termination from fabrication/unverified repetition), R371 (exhaustive gate enumeration before declaring fix complete), PR192 (stabilizing step first). The deployed files are: dispatcher_v2.py (adds coms-pending gate with anti-deadlock logic), coms_watch_v2.py (sets/clears coms-pending.bin), hook.py (COMS catch-up on SessionStart + UserPromptSubmit). The coms-pending.bin files were initialized to '0'.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall establishes three failure modes relevant here: (1) E263 — fabrication under pressure, wrong binary path repeated confidently without verify. (2) R371 — partial fixes masquerade as complete when not all gates are enumerated. (3) E265 — pipeline integration failures happen at boundaries (wrong model dispatched, stale state carried forward). The specific risk: coms_watch_v2.py must actually monitor and set the binary; the dispatcher must actually read it; hook.py must actually trigger the catch-up path. Compilation confirms syntax, not semantics.
hypotheses.develop: Recall-grounded verification protocol: cross-reference the three deployed components against their stated contracts. dispatcher_v2.py contract: reads coms-pending.bin, gates on its value, has anti-deadlock. coms_watch_v2.py contract: sets coms-pending.bin to '1' when pending, clears to '0' after delivery. hook.py contract: on SessionStart + UserPromptSubmit, triggers COMS catch-up. Minimum viable proof: service active + binary readable + at least one log line showing v2 behavior.
hypotheses.deliver: Verify against the three component contracts: [Gate existence] `grep -n 'coms.pending' ~/Khaan/brain/engines/dispatcher.py | head -20` — confirm gate logic present. [Watch contract] `grep -n 'coms-pending' ~/Khaan/brain/engines/coms_watch.py | head -20` — confirm set/clear logic present. [Hook wiring] `grep -n 'COMS\|coms' ~/Khaan/wiring/hook.py | head -20` — confirm catch-up trigger present. [Live binary] `cat ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin` — confirm '0'. [Service] `systemctl status brain-coms-watch --no-pager | head -15`. Report all five raw outputs.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Contrarian read: Justs already knows 'ALL CLEAN' came back. He ran the commands himself. He is NOT asking for a compilation re-confirm. He may be implicitly challenging whether these v2 files are the right fix at all — not whether they compile. The deeper question: does the coms-pending gate design actually solve the problem it was meant to solve (presumably: messages received while Kha'an is mid-session were being dropped or deadlocked), and does the anti-deadlock logic correctly handle the edge case it targets?
hypotheses.diagnose: If the contrarian read is correct, a purely mechanical verify (service running + binary = 0) misses the point entirely. Justs may want functional proof: can the system receive a COMS signal, set the pending bit, and have the dispatcher defer correctly without deadlocking? The risk of the literal verify: it produces green lights on infrastructure while leaving the semantic correctness unprobed. The word 'verify' after a complex multi-file deployment historically means 'prove it actually does what it's supposed to do.'
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial test design: identify the specific failure mode the v2 patch was meant to fix, then probe whether it's actually fixed. If coms-pending gate prevents deadlock by deferring dispatch when a message is pending, the test must simulate a pending state and confirm dispatcher defers. If coms_watch sets the bit on incoming message and clears after delivery, the test must show the bit transitions. This is beyond compilation — it requires behavioral observation.
hypotheses.deliver: Probe functional semantics: (1) Manually set coms-pending to '1': `echo -n '1' > ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin`. (2) Attempt to trigger dispatcher and observe whether it gates: `python3 -c "import sys; sys.path.insert(0,'~/Khaan'); from brain.engines.dispatcher import dispatch; print(dispatch('test'))" 2>&1 | head -10`. (3) Confirm dispatcher respected the gate (deferred, not executed). (4) Reset: `echo -n '0' > ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin`. (5) Confirm coms_watch log shows expected transitions. If dispatcher has no callable API surface, read source for gate branch and confirm code path is reachable.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest action that satisfies 'verify': confirm the three most critical post-deployment facts — (1) brain-coms-watch service is active, (2) coms-pending.bin files hold '0' as initialized, (3) no import errors in deployed Python files beyond what py_compile already confirmed. Everything else (functional gate testing, behavioral probes) is scope creep unless those three fail.
hypotheses.diagnose: Justs ran python3 -m py_compile on all five files and got ALL CLEAN. He restarted the service himself. The minimal gap is: did the service come up cleanly after restart, and are the binary files intact? Those two facts are unconfirmed. Everything else — semantic correctness, gate behavior, log analysis — is second-order. Risk of over-verification: consuming time and returning a wall of output to a user with zero patience for verbosity.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor: two commands, two facts, one response. Keep output scannable. If both pass, declare verified with evidence. If either fails, that is the finding — escalate only that. Do not pad with additional checks unless a failure surfaces. Justs reads results, not methodology.
hypotheses.deliver: Two commands, report inline: `systemctl is-active brain-coms-watch && echo SERVICE_OK || echo SERVICE_DOWN` — expect SERVICE_OK. `cat ~/Khaan/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin && echo '' && cat ~/Khaan/jeff/brain/binaries/coms-pending.bin` — expect '0' twice. If both pass: 'Verified. Service active, both pending bins at 0.' If either fails: report the raw failure output only, no diagnosis until asked.
